Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic most of the time. I work part-time to support myself, so I had to squeeze in studying whenever I could—sometimes early mornings before work, sometimes late at night. I tried to set aside about 15 hours a week, but it rarely happened in a structured way. I learned to listen to lecture recordings while commuting or doing chores, which helped me absorb material even when I couldn't sit down properly. It wasn't ideal, but I made it work with what I had.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what shifts I had at work. Some weeks I'd be consistent, studying a bit each day, but then a busy period at work would throw everything off. Deadlines were really what drove my schedule—I'd cram more intensely when something was due. I wished I could be more regular, but life just didn't allow for that kind of stability. Being 28 and juggling responsibilities, you learn to be flexible.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or a notification from a classmate in our peer support group that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd feel anxious about falling behind and that fear would push me to check what I'd missed. I also logged in when I needed to download lecture recordings since I learn best by listening. The fear of failing after coming this far in my education was a big motivator, even if it wasn't the healthiest one.
Q4: There was a stretch mid-semester where logging in became more routine for me. My peer support group started doing weekly check-ins, and that accountability really helped. I'd log in to post in discussions and see what others were struggling with—it made me feel less alone. But honestly, that routine faded when things got overwhelming with work and stress. The peer connection was what made it feel natural rather than forced.
Q5: The lecture recordings were absolutely essential for me—being an auditory learner, I could replay explanations until they clicked. Discussion forums were also valuable because connecting with peers helped me understand concepts from different angles. When someone in my peer support group explained something their way, it often made more sense than the textbook. The videos I could watch at my own pace were helpful too, especially the ones I could listen to while doing other things.
Q6: I'll admit I often skipped the longer reading materials and some of the supplementary PDFs. With limited time and resources, I had to prioritize what would help me pass, and dense readings took too much time for what I got out of them. Some of the interactive quizzes I also delayed because they felt like extra work when I was already struggling to complete the main assignments. I know I probably missed out on deeper learning, but survival mode kicked in.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to passing the course or understanding something I was genuinely confused about. If a peer recommended something or if it was clearly going to be on the exam, I'd engage with it fully. I also valued anything I could learn from while multitasking—like audio content. When I'm scared about failing, I focus on what's most likely to help me survive academically.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. Required ones got my attention because they directly impacted my grade, and with my assignment completion being lower than I wanted, I couldn't risk ignoring them. Optional stuff felt like it was designed for students with more time and fewer worries. Sometimes I felt guilty about skipping them, but I had to be realistic about my capacity.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone during my commute or while cooking. I also had a study buddy from my peer support group, and we'd meet at the library to quiz each other verbally. I took handwritten notes that I'd review away from the computer. My internet isn't always reliable at home, so offline studying was often more practical anyway.
Q10: There were many times I was genuinely learning but the LMS wouldn't show it. Like when I'd listen to a downloaded lecture three or four times while working, really letting it sink in. Or when my peer support group would meet in person and we'd spend hours discussing concepts. I also did a lot of thinking and processing during quiet moments—on the bus, before sleep. That mental engagement doesn't leave a digital trace.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my main strategy. I'd access a video or document once, download it, and then study from it repeatedly offline. With my limited resources and unreliable internet, it made more sense to grab everything I needed in one session. I'd return to those downloaded materials for days or even weeks. The LMS would only show that single access, but I was engaging with that content much more deeply than the logs suggested.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods, especially when my stress levels peaked. Around midterms, I was working extra shifts and barely sleeping—I felt scared that I wouldn't make it through. My anxiety made it hard to even open the LMS because I dreaded seeing how behind I was. Financial pressures were constant background noise that made focusing on coursework feel almost impossible sometimes. The peer support helped pull me back, but those moments were really hard.
Q13: All of those factors affected me significantly. Fatigue was probably the biggest one—after a long shift, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without absorbing anything. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules entirely for a while. Work responsibilities meant I'd sometimes go days without properly engaging with course material. I learned to forgive myself for these lapses, but they definitely showed up in my completion rates.
Q14: Yes, more often than I'd like to admit. Sometimes I'd have the LMS open while I was too exhausted to actually focus. I'd scroll through content or watch a video but realize afterward that none of it stuck. Those were frustrating moments because I was trying, but my brain just wasn't cooperating. The fear and anxiety sometimes made it worse—I'd be so worried about failing that I couldn't actually concentrate on learning.
Q15: I postponed assignments regularly, even knowing they were important. Part of it was feeling overwhelmed and not knowing where to start. Part of it was fear—if I didn't start, I couldn't fail at it yet. Sometimes I'd avoid difficult topics because my confidence was low and I was scared of confirming that I couldn't do it. The peer support group helped me push through some of this, but procrastination was definitely a pattern I struggled with.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find energy I didn't know I had. My LMS activity would spike dramatically in the days before something was due. It wasn't healthy, but that pressure was what got things done. Without deadlines, I'd probably drift and focus on more immediate life concerns. They created the urgency I needed to prioritize coursework over everything else temporarily.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback gave me a boost and made me want to engage more—it felt like validation that I could do this. But critical feedback sometimes triggered my anxiety and made me want to avoid that area of the course. When peers in my support group gave me encouragement, that was often more motivating than formal feedback. I needed to feel like someone believed in me to keep going.
Q18: The course structure was somewhat flexible, which was both good and bad for me. Good because I could work around my job schedule, bad because without rigid structure, I'd let things slide. Weekly modules gave me some rhythm to follow. The peer support intervention was actually crucial—having that structured social connection made me feel accountable. Without it, I think I would have disengaged much more.
Q19: The availability of recorded lectures made engagement much easier for someone like me who learns by listening. However, the heavy reading requirements were harder given my limited time. Discussion forums were accessible, but I sometimes felt intimidated posting when I wasn't sure I understood the material correctly. More audio-based content and clearer guidance on priorities would have helped me engage more effectively with my constraints.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying hard but struggling to produce quality work. They might be watching videos, reading materials, and participating in discussions, but when it comes to assignments, they freeze up or run out of time. Fear of failure can make you engage with content without actually submitting anything. Or maybe life circumstances prevent them from completing work even though they're genuinely trying to learn.
Q21: That actually resonates with my experience somewhat. I studied a lot offline—listening to recordings, discussing with peers, reviewing handwritten notes. My exam score was decent enough to pass even though my LMS activity was low. Some people just learn differently or have circumstances that push their studying outside the digital space. The LMS doesn't capture everything, especially for students like me who download and study independently.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone logs in, what they click on, how long they spend on pages. But they miss the quality of engagement and all the learning that happens offline. They can't capture a student listening to a lecture recording five times on their phone, or a peer study session at a coffee shop. They also miss the emotional context—whether someone is engaged out of genuine interest or desperate fear. The numbers tell a very incomplete story.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions to pass. It's when I'm actively thinking about concepts, connecting them to things I already know, and wanting to learn more. For me, it also involves feeling connected to peers and not completely alone in the struggle. True engagement is when learning feels meaningful, even when it's hard. It's not just about time spent—it's about mental and emotional investment.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I was most of the semester, or how much the peer support intervention meant to me emotionally. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent listening to lectures while working or commuting. It can't see the growth in my confidence when a peer told me I explained something well. My resilience—getting back up after every setback—doesn't show in login data. The human side of my learning journey is completely invisible in those numbers.
